title: How to Safely Approach a Stray Dog
content:
1. Stay calm and move slowly. Avoid sudden movements or loud noises.
2. Turn your body slightly sideways — facing a dog head-on can feel threatening.
3. Avoid direct eye contact. Look at the dog's body, not its eyes.
4. Crouch down to appear smaller and less threatening.
5. Extend the back of your hand slowly for the dog to sniff.
6. Let the dog come to you — never chase or corner a stray.
7. Watch the tail: a low, slow wag is cautious; tucked tail means fear; stiff upright tail means alert/agitated.
8. If the dog growls or backs away, stop and give it space. Do not force interaction.

title: Basic First Aid for Bleeding Wounds
content:
1. If you can safely approach the dog, wear gloves if available.
2. Apply gentle pressure with a clean cloth to stop bleeding.
3. Do NOT try to remove objects embedded in a wound.
4. Keep the wound clean — rinse gently with clean water if available.
5. Do NOT apply human antiseptics like Dettol or hydrogen peroxide — they can harm dogs.
6. Wrap loosely with a clean cloth bandage.
7. Get the dog to a vet as soon as possible. Call a rescue helpline if you can't transport.
8. For heavy bleeding that won't stop, apply firm pressure and rush to emergency vet.

title: Helping a Dog with Mange (Skin Disease)
content:
Mange is very common in Mumbai's strays. Signs: patches of hair loss, red/irritated skin, excessive scratching, crusty/scaly skin.

1. Do NOT apply home remedies like kerosene, engine oil, or turmeric paste — these are harmful myths.
2. Mange is treatable. Contact a local NGO or vet for proper Ivermectin treatment.
3. You can help by providing nutritious food (rice + boiled chicken/egg) to build immunity.
4. Mange treatment takes 2-3 months. The dog needs consistent medication.
5. Sarcoptic mange is contagious to humans (temporary rash). Wash hands after contact.
6. Demodectic mange is NOT contagious but indicates low immunity.
7. Report severely mangy dogs to WSD, IDA, or World For All for treatment.

title: What to Do If You Find a Hit-by-Vehicle Dog
content:
Road accidents are a leading cause of injury for Mumbai's strays.

1. SAFETY FIRST: Make sure the road is safe before approaching.
2. Do NOT move the dog unless it's in immediate danger of being hit again.
3. If you must move it, slide a flat board or stiff cloth under the dog to use as a stretcher.
4. Do NOT pick up the dog by its limbs — there may be internal injuries or fractures.
5. Keep the dog warm with a cloth or blanket.
6. Call the nearest 24hr emergency vet or rescue helpline immediately.
7. If the dog is conscious and in pain, approach from behind slowly. An injured dog may bite out of pain.
8. Stay with the dog until help arrives if possible — your presence is comforting.
9. Note the exact location (Google Maps pin) so rescuers can find you.

title: Dehydration and Heatstroke in Dogs
content:
Mumbai's heat is brutal for strays, especially in April-June.

Signs of dehydration: dry gums, sunken eyes, lethargy, loss of skin elasticity.
Signs of heatstroke: excessive panting, drooling, vomiting, collapse, bright red tongue.

1. Offer clean, cool (not ice cold) water immediately.
2. Move the dog to shade.
3. Wet the dog's paws and belly with cool water. Do NOT douse with ice water.
4. Place wet towels on the neck and armpits.
5. Fan the dog if possible.
6. If the dog won't drink, wet its lips and gums with water.
7. Get to a vet urgently for severe heatstroke — it can be fatal within minutes.
8. Prevention: Keep water bowls outside your building for strays. An earthen pot (matka) keeps water cool.

title: Feeding Stray Dogs Safely
content:
Thank you for feeding strays! Here's how to do it responsibly:

1. Feed at the same time and place daily — this reduces roaming and conflicts.
2. Good foods: rice with boiled chicken/egg, roti with dal, curd rice, dog kibble.
3. Avoid: chocolate, onion, garlic, grapes, raisins, cooked bones (they splinter), very spicy food.
4. Always provide water alongside food.
5. Clean up leftover food after 20-30 minutes to avoid attracting rats.
6. Feed in groups if possible — community feeding reduces territorial aggression.
7. If a dog seems to have difficulty eating, check for mouth injuries or dental issues.
8. Puppies under 4 weeks need milk (Lactol or diluted cow milk), NOT buffalo milk.

title: Rabies: What You Need to Know
content:
India accounts for 36% of global rabies deaths. Awareness saves lives.

1. Rabies is 100% fatal once symptoms appear, but 100% preventable with timely vaccination.
2. ANY dog bite or scratch that breaks skin needs immediate action.
3. Wash the wound thoroughly with soap and running water for 15 minutes.
4. Apply povidone-iodine (Betadine) if available.
5. Go to the nearest hospital for post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) — anti-rabies vaccine.
6. Do NOT apply chilli, turmeric, or other folk remedies.
7. Complete the full course of vaccines (usually 4-5 doses over a month).
8. Report the biting dog to local authorities for observation (do NOT kill the dog).
9. Vaccinated stray dogs (identifiable by an ear notch) are much lower risk, but still get PEP.
10. Signs of rabies in dogs: excessive drooling, aggression, disorientation, paralysis, fear of water.

title: Tick and Flea Infestation
content:
Ticks are extremely common on Mumbai strays, especially during monsoon.

1. Large ticks are visible to the naked eye — check ears, neck, between toes, and groin area.
2. Do NOT pull ticks with bare fingers — use tweezers and pull straight out, close to the skin.
3. Heavy tick infestations can cause anaemia (pale gums, lethargy, weakness).
4. A vet can provide Fipronil spray or Bravecto tablets for treatment.
5. For community dogs you feed regularly, ask a vet about monthly preventive treatment.
6. Tick fever (Ehrlichiosis) is a serious complication — symptoms include fever, loss of appetite, bleeding from nose.
7. If you suspect tick fever, rush to a vet immediately. It's treatable but can be fatal if delayed.

title: Caring for Orphaned Puppies
content:
Finding orphaned puppies is common. Here's how to help:

1. FIRST: Make sure the mother is truly gone. Mother dogs often leave pups to forage and return. Wait 4-6 hours and observe.
2. If pups are cold, crying continuously, or look thin, they likely need help.
3. Keep puppies warm — a hot water bottle wrapped in cloth, or a warm (not hot) pad.
4. Feed with a small syringe or pet nursing bottle. Use Lactol powder mixed with warm water.
5. Never feed cow or buffalo milk directly — it causes diarrhoea. Dilute with equal parts water if nothing else is available.
6. Feed every 2-3 hours for newborns, every 4 hours for pups over 2 weeks.
7. After feeding, gently rub the puppy's belly and genital area with a warm damp cloth to stimulate urination/defecation.
8. Contact a rescue NGO for proper fostering — hand-raising neonatal pups is very difficult alone.

title: Dog Body Language Guide
content:
Understanding body language keeps both you and the dog safe.

HAPPY/FRIENDLY: Relaxed body, wagging tail (wide sweeps), soft eyes, open mouth (like a smile), may play-bow (front down, rear up).

SCARED/ANXIOUS: Tail tucked between legs, ears flat, body low/crouching, lip licking, yawning (stress signal), may try to flee. A scared dog can bite if cornered.

AGGRESSIVE/THREATENED: Stiff body, raised hackles (fur along spine), showing teeth, direct stare, growling, forward-leaning posture. GIVE SPACE immediately.

SUBMISSIVE: Rolling on back showing belly, avoiding eye contact, ears back, tail low and possibly wagging slightly. This dog is not a threat but is very nervous.

IN PAIN: Whimpering, limping, licking a specific area obsessively, reluctance to move, snapping when touched (pain response, not aggression).

title: Monsoon Health Risks for Stray Dogs
content:
Mumbai's monsoon (June-September) creates unique health risks for strays.

1. Waterlogged areas breed mosquitoes — dogs can get heartworm and leptospirosis.
2. Wet conditions worsen skin infections, fungal infections, and ear infections.
3. Stagnant water carries bacteria — dogs drinking from puddles may get gastro infections.
4. Many strays drown in flooded areas or open drains during heavy rains.
5. How to help: Provide dry shelters (even a raised platform with a tarp), clean water, and food.
6. If you see a dog with persistent diarrhoea during monsoon, it may have lepto — get it to a vet.
7. After floods, watch for dogs showing limping or wound infections from contaminated water.

title: Legal Rights of Stray Dogs in India
content:
Stray dogs are protected under Indian law.

1. The Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Act, 1960 makes it illegal to harm, poison, or kill stray dogs.
2. ABC Rules, 2023 mandate that stray dogs can only be sterilized and vaccinated, NOT relocated or killed.
3. Feeding strays is NOT illegal. No housing society can ban feeding. (Supreme Court and various High Court orders)
4. Relocating strays from their territory is illegal under ABC Rules.
5. If someone harms a stray, you can file an FIR under Section 428/429 IPC (now BNS 2023 equivalent).
6. Report animal cruelty to: local police, AWBI (Animal Welfare Board of India), or PFA (People for Animals).
7. Municipal corporations are legally required to run ABC programs.

title: Distemper in Stray Dogs
content:
Canine distemper is common and often fatal in unvaccinated strays.

Signs: Fever, nasal discharge, coughing, lethargy, loss of appetite, vomiting, diarrhoea. In later stages: seizures, muscle twitching, hardened paw pads.

1. Distemper is viral — there is NO cure, only supportive care.
2. If you suspect distemper, keep the dog isolated from other dogs (it's highly contagious).
3. Get to a vet immediately for supportive treatment (fluids, anti-nausea, antibiotics for secondary infections).
4. Many dogs survive distemper with good nursing care. Don't give up.
5. Dogs that survive may have lifelong neurological twitches — this is normal.
6. Prevention: ABC programs include vaccination. Support your local ABC NGO.

title: How to Report Animal Cruelty
content:
If you witness animal cruelty in Mumbai:

1. Document with photos/videos from a safe distance.
2. Note the location, time, and description of the perpetrator.
3. File a complaint at the nearest police station. They are legally required to register it.
4. Call AWBI helpline: 1962 (national animal helpline)
5. Contact local NGOs: WSD (+91-22-2307-0773), BSPCA (+91-22-2415-0774)
6. You can also file a complaint online through the AWBI portal.
7. Under BNS 2023, animal cruelty can lead to fines and imprisonment.
8. For poisoning cases, try to identify the substance and inform the vet treating the dog.
9. You have the right to take the animal to a vet for treatment even before legal proceedings.

title: What to Do If a Dog Won't Eat
content:
Loss of appetite in a stray dog can indicate several issues.

1. First, check if the dog can physically eat — look for mouth injuries, broken teeth, or swelling.
2. Try offering strong-smelling food like boiled chicken or wet dog food.
3. If the dog has vomiting/diarrhoea along with appetite loss, it could be parvovirus (especially in puppies), distemper, or gastro issues — see a vet urgently.
4. In hot weather, reduced appetite is normal. Ensure water is available.
5. A dog that hasn't eaten for more than 24 hours needs veterinary attention.
6. Dehydration from not eating/drinking can become critical quickly — look for dry gums and skin tenting.

title: Dog Limping or Unable to Walk
content:
A limping stray dog needs careful assessment.

1. Observe from a distance first — is the dog putting weight on the leg at all? No weight = possible fracture.
2. Check the paw pads for glass, thorns, nails, or cuts. Mumbai streets are full of sharp debris.
3. If you can handle the dog safely, gently feel along the leg for swelling, heat, or unusual angles.
4. Do NOT try to set or splint a broken bone yourself — you can cause more damage.
5. For a suspected fracture, keep the dog still and calm. Use a board or stiff cardboard as a stretcher.
6. A limp that comes and goes may be a sprain — rest and anti-inflammatory treatment from a vet helps.
7. Chronic limping in older dogs could be arthritis. A vet can prescribe pain management.
8. If the paw is swollen between the toes, it could be an abscess from a foreign body — needs vet drainage.
9. Contact your nearest rescue NGO for transport if you cannot take the dog to a vet yourself.

title: Eye Injuries and Infections in Dogs
content:
Eye problems are extremely common in street dogs.

1. Signs: redness, swelling, discharge (clear, yellow, or green), squinting, pawing at the eye, cloudiness.
2. Green/yellow discharge usually means bacterial infection — needs antibiotic eye drops from a vet.
3. Clear watery discharge may be irritation from dust, pollution, or allergies.
4. Do NOT touch the eye or try to remove any object stuck in the eye — let a vet handle it.
5. If the eye is bulging or looks like it might come out (proptosis from trauma), keep it moist with saline or clean water and rush to a vet.
6. Cherry eye (red bulge in corner of eye) is common in puppies — it's not painful but needs surgical correction.
7. Cloudy/blue eyes in older dogs may indicate cataracts or glaucoma.
8. You can gently clean around the eye with a soft cloth and warm water to remove crusted discharge.
9. Many one-eyed strays live perfectly normal lives after losing an eye to infection or injury.

title: Dog Caught in Wire, Rope, or Trap
content:
Dogs frequently get caught in wire, kite string (manja), ropes, and discarded netting in Mumbai.

1. Manja (kite string) cuts are a major problem — the nylon/glass-coated string cuts deep into skin, especially the neck and legs.
2. If a dog has a wire/string wound, do NOT pull it out — it may have cut into tissue and pulling causes more damage.
3. Approach calmly. A trapped dog is terrified and may bite. Use thick gloves or a towel to protect your hands.
4. If the entanglement is loose, carefully cut it free with scissors, keeping your fingers clear of the dog's mouth.
5. For deeply embedded wire/manja, do NOT attempt removal — stabilize the dog and get to a vet for surgical removal.
6. Cover the wound with a clean cloth to prevent flies and further contamination.
7. Report locations with dangerous loose wires/manja to your local municipal office.
8. Rubber bands and hair ties around a dog's muzzle or legs are also common — these cut off circulation and need urgent removal.

title: Poisoning in Stray Dogs
content:
Intentional and accidental poisoning kills many stray dogs in India.

Signs: sudden vomiting, excessive drooling, foaming at the mouth, seizures, tremors, difficulty breathing, blue gums, collapse.

1. If you suspect poisoning, TIME IS CRITICAL — get to a vet within 30 minutes if possible.
2. Do NOT try to make the dog vomit unless a vet specifically tells you to (some poisons cause more damage coming back up).
3. Try to identify what the dog ate — rat poison, pesticide, antifreeze, and certain plants are common causes.
4. Rat poison (common in Mumbai buildings) causes internal bleeding — symptoms may be delayed 2-3 days.
5. If you see blue/green pellets or suspicious food left out for dogs, collect a sample for the vet (wear gloves).
6. Keep the dog warm and calm during transport to the vet.
7. Intentional poisoning of dogs is a criminal offense under PCA Act and BNS 2023 — file an FIR.
8. Document the location and any evidence of deliberate poisoning for legal proceedings.
9. Common household toxins: chocolate, xylitol (sugar-free gum), onions, grapes, certain medications.

title: Dog Fight Wounds and Bite Injuries
content:
Dog fights are common among territorial strays. Bite wounds are more serious than they look.

1. Puncture wounds from bites are deceptive — the surface hole is small but damage goes deep into tissue.
2. Do NOT just clean the surface and assume it's fine. Bite wounds almost always get infected without treatment.
3. If you can safely handle the dog, clean the wound with saline solution (1 tsp salt in 1 litre boiled/cooled water).
4. Look for multiple puncture wounds — dogs often bite repeatedly during fights.
5. Swelling appearing 2-3 days after a fight usually means an abscess forming — needs vet drainage and antibiotics.
6. Bite wounds near the neck, chest, or abdomen can be life-threatening due to internal damage.
7. A dog that was in a fight may be in pain and snap — approach from behind and use a towel to handle.
8. If two dogs are actively fighting, do NOT put your hands between them. Make a loud noise, spray water, or throw a blanket over them.
9. After treatment, keep the wound clean and watch for signs of infection: increased swelling, heat, foul smell.

title: Ear Infections and Ear Problems
content:
Ear problems cause significant discomfort for street dogs, especially during monsoon.

1. Signs: constant head shaking, tilting head to one side, scratching at ears, dark/smelly discharge, redness inside the ear flap.
2. Ear mites cause dark, crumbly discharge and intense itching — highly contagious between dogs.
3. Bacterial/yeast infections cause wet, smelly discharge — common in monsoon due to moisture.
4. Do NOT push anything into the ear canal (no cotton buds!) — you can rupture the eardrum.
5. You can gently clean the outer ear flap with a soft damp cloth.
6. A persistent head tilt may indicate a deep ear infection affecting balance — needs urgent vet care.
7. Fly-bitten ear tips (common in summer) cause bleeding, crusting, and pain. Clean gently and apply petroleum jelly to deter flies.
8. Dogs with floppy ears are more prone to infections due to reduced air circulation.
9. A vet will typically prescribe ear drops (antibiotic + antifungal + steroid) for 7-14 days.

title: Parvovirus in Puppies
content:
Parvovirus is the #1 killer of unvaccinated puppies. Fast action saves lives.

Signs: severe vomiting, bloody foul-smelling diarrhoea, extreme lethargy, refusal to eat/drink, rapid dehydration.

1. Parvo is an EMERGENCY — survival drops dramatically without treatment within 24-48 hours.
2. Puppies aged 6 weeks to 6 months are most vulnerable.
3. The virus is extremely contagious — keep infected puppies isolated from all other dogs.
4. The virus survives in the environment for months. Clean contaminated areas with bleach solution (1:30 bleach to water).
5. Treatment is supportive: IV fluids, anti-nausea meds, antibiotics for secondary infections. There is no antiviral cure.
6. With aggressive vet treatment, survival rate is 70-90%. Without treatment, mortality is 90%+.
7. Do NOT give the puppy food or water if it's actively vomiting — this makes it worse. The vet will give IV fluids.
8. After recovery, the puppy will have immunity but should still complete vaccinations.
9. Prevention: vaccination at 6, 8, and 12 weeks. Support ABC programs that vaccinate street puppies.

title: Burns and Scalding Injuries
content:
Burns from hot water, chemicals, or fire are unfortunately common in stray dogs.

1. For thermal burns (hot water/oil): immediately cool the area with running cool (not ice cold) water for 10-15 minutes.
2. Do NOT apply ice, butter, toothpaste, or any home remedy — these trap heat and worsen the burn.
3. For chemical burns (acid/alkali): flush the area with large amounts of clean water for at least 20 minutes.
4. Do NOT try to neutralize chemicals (e.g., putting alkali on acid burns) — just flush with water.
5. Cover the burn loosely with a clean, non-fluffy cloth (cling wrap works well if available).
6. Burns are extremely painful — the dog may bite. Approach with care and use a muzzle if possible.
7. Even small burns can become seriously infected. Get to a vet for proper wound management.
8. Large burns (more than 10% of body) are life-threatening and need emergency vet care with IV fluids.
9. Intentional burning of animals is a serious criminal offense — document and report to police immediately.

title: Choking or Difficulty Breathing
content:
A choking dog needs quick assessment but careful action.

1. Signs of choking: pawing at mouth, gagging, drooling, panic, blue/grey gums (no oxygen), inability to bark.
2. If the dog can still breathe partially (coughing), let it try to dislodge the object itself. Do NOT reach into the mouth.
3. For complete airway blockage: open the mouth carefully and look inside. If you can SEE the object, try to sweep it out with your finger (risk of being bitten).
4. For small dogs: hold upside down by the hind legs and give 4-5 firm back blows between the shoulder blades.
5. For large dogs: stand behind, place fist just below the rib cage, and give 5 quick upward thrusts (like human Heimlich).
6. Common choking hazards for strays: bones (especially cooked chicken bones), plastic, stones, food wrappers.
7. After dislodging an object, check for injuries inside the mouth.
8. Difficulty breathing WITHOUT choking (wheezing, laboured breathing) could be pneumonia, heart disease, or allergic reaction — see a vet.
9. Blue or white gums mean critically low oxygen — this is a life-threatening emergency.

title: Dealing with Maggot Wounds (Myiasis)
content:
Maggot-infested wounds are one of the most common conditions in Mumbai's strays, especially in summer.

1. Maggots come from fly eggs laid in open wounds. They eat dead tissue but also damage healthy tissue.
2. If you spot maggots in a wound, the dog NEEDS veterinary treatment — do not delay.
3. While waiting for vet help, you can pour saline solution (salt water) on the wound to drive out some maggots.
4. Do NOT use turpentine, kerosene, or chemicals to kill maggots — these damage tissue and cause chemical burns.
5. You CAN carefully remove visible maggots with tweezers if the dog is calm and cooperative.
6. The wound needs thorough cleaning, maggot removal, possibly debridement, and antibiotics.
7. Cover the wound loosely to prevent flies from laying more eggs.
8. After treatment, the wound needs daily cleaning and bandage changes for 1-2 weeks.
9. Prevention: any open wound on a stray should be treated promptly before flies can lay eggs.
10. Contact Bombay SPCA, WSD, or Blue Cross for free maggot wound treatment.

title: Helping a Dog Stuck Somewhere
content:
Dogs (especially puppies) frequently get stuck in drains, fences, gates, and narrow spaces in Mumbai.

1. Stay calm and speak softly to the dog — a panicked dog will struggle harder and injure itself more.
2. Assess the situation: Is the dog in immediate danger (water rising, traffic, etc.)?
3. For a dog stuck in a fence or gate: try lubricating the area with cooking oil or soap to help slide it through.
4. Do NOT pull the dog forcefully — you can cause fractures, dislocations, or internal injuries.
5. For dogs trapped in drains/pits: call the fire brigade (101) — they handle animal rescues.
6. BMC ward offices are legally required to assist with animal rescue situations.
7. While waiting for help, provide shade and water if possible.
8. If you can see injuries from the entrapment, don't try to treat them on-site — let rescuers handle extraction.
9. Document the location and share with local animal rescue groups on social media for fastest response.
10. Rescue organizations: WSD, YODA, Bombay SPCA, or call AWBI helpline 1962.

title: Pregnant or Nursing Stray Dogs
content:
Pregnant and nursing mother dogs need extra support.

1. A pregnant stray needs extra nutrition — feed protein-rich food (boiled eggs, chicken, paneer) 2-3 times a day.
2. Provide a safe, sheltered whelping area — a quiet corner with a cardboard box lined with old clothes.
3. Most dogs deliver without problems, but watch for signs of distress: straining for more than 1 hour without producing a puppy, green/black discharge before first pup, lethargy.
4. Do NOT disturb a mother during or just after delivery — she may reject or harm puppies if stressed.
5. Nursing mothers need 2-3x their normal food intake. Dehydration from milk production is common.
6. If a mother dies or rejects puppies, see our guide on caring for orphaned puppies.
7. Get the mother sterilized through ABC program after puppies are weaned (8 weeks).
8. Watch for signs of mastitis (infected mammary glands): swollen, hot, painful teats with possible discharge.
9. Never separate puppies from their mother before 8 weeks of age unless there's a medical emergency.

title: Skin Infections and Hot Spots
content:
Skin infections are the most common health issue in Mumbai's strays due to heat and humidity.

1. Hot spots: moist, red, painful patches of skin that spread quickly. Caused by bacteria, often triggered by an underlying itch.
2. Ringworm: circular patches of hair loss with crusty edges. It's a FUNGAL infection (not a worm) and can spread to humans.
3. Bacterial skin infections: red bumps, pus-filled blisters, crusting, hair loss with foul smell.
4. For hot spots: if you can handle the dog, trim hair around the area and clean with dilute chlorhexidine (Savlon).
5. Keep the area dry — moisture makes bacterial and fungal infections worse.
6. Do NOT apply human antifungal creams without vet advice — some are toxic to dogs if licked.
7. Ringworm treatment takes 4-6 weeks of consistent antifungal medication.
8. Chronic skin issues often indicate underlying problems: poor nutrition, immune deficiency, or hormonal issues.
9. Maintaining good nutrition is the single best way to prevent skin infections in strays you regularly feed.
10. Wash your hands after handling dogs with skin infections, especially ringworm.

title: What to Do About Aggressive Stray Dogs
content:
Most stray dogs are NOT aggressive, but some situations can trigger defensive behavior.

1. Dogs most commonly show aggression when: protecting food, guarding puppies, defending territory, in pain, or feeling cornered.
2. If a dog is barking at you: STOP walking. Stand still sideways. Avoid eye contact. Let the dog lose interest.
3. Do NOT run from an aggressive dog — running triggers the chase instinct and makes things worse.
4. If a dog chases you: stop, stand your ground, and shout firmly 'NO' or 'GO'. Most dogs stop when you stop.
5. Carry a stick or umbrella while walking in areas with aggressive strays — holding (not waving) it is usually enough to deter.
6. Dogs that suddenly become aggressive after being friendly may be in pain or developing rabies — inform local animal control.
7. Night aggression is more common — dogs become more territorial after dark. Carry a torch/flashlight.
8. Pack behavior: if multiple dogs are threatening, back away slowly. Do not turn and run.
9. Report genuinely dangerous dogs to the local ABC centre — they can be sterilized and vaccinated, which reduces aggression.
10. Remember: aggression almost always comes from fear or pain, not malice.

title: Diarrhoea and Stomach Problems
content:
Digestive issues are extremely common in strays due to scavenging.

1. Mild diarrhoea (loose but not watery, no blood): often self-resolving. Offer bland food — plain rice with boiled chicken.
2. Bloody or black diarrhoea: URGENT — could be parvovirus, poisoning, or severe infection. See a vet immediately.
3. Continuous vomiting + diarrhoea together causes rapid dehydration — this is an emergency, especially in puppies.
4. Worms are a major cause of chronic diarrhoea in strays. A bloated belly with visible ribs usually means heavy worm load.
5. Deworming is cheap and effective — a vet can provide a single dose of deworming tablet.
6. ORS (Oral Rehydration Solution) or Electral mixed with water can help prevent dehydration in mild cases.
7. Do NOT give human anti-diarrhoeal medicines like Imodium to dogs without vet advice.
8. Chronic on-and-off diarrhoea may indicate inflammatory bowel disease, food intolerance, or persistent parasites.
9. If you regularly feed a group of strays, coordinate with a vet for regular deworming every 3 months.
10. Always have clean water available — dehydration from diarrhoea kills more puppies than the infection itself.
